skill.name: doc-coauthoring
skill.description: Guide users through a structured workflow for co-authoring documentation. Use when user wants to write documentation, proposals, technical specs, decision docs, or similar structured content.
skill.summary: A three-stage collaborative workflow for creating high-quality documentation with AI assistance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The 'external_commands' references are Claude Code tool calls (create_file, str_replace), not shell execution. The 'network' reference is documentation text directing users to claude.ai, not actual network calls. Cryptographic and reconnaissance patterns are text matches in documentation context only. This is a pure instructional skill with no executable security risks.

content.user_title: Co-author documentation with structured AI guidance
content.value_statement: Creating quality documentation requires iteration and fresh perspectives. This skill provides a proven three-stage workflow that transforms vague ideas into clear, reader-tested documents.
actual_capabilities: Guide users through a structured three-stage documentation workflow
Ask clarifying questions to gather complete context before drafting
Build documents section-by-section with iterative refinement
Perform reader testing to identify gaps and ambiguities
Create and edit files using Claude Code tool integrations
Adapt to different document types including specs, proposals, and RFCs
limitations: Requires user participation throughout the multi-stage process
Reader testing requires either sub-agent access or manual fresh session testing
Does not automatically fetch external documents without user-provided integrations
Best suited for substantial documents rather than quick notes

use_cases.title: Technical Specification Writing
use_cases.description: Engineers creating technical specs can use this workflow to systematically capture requirements, explore alternatives, and produce reader-ready documentation that anticipates implementation questions.

use_cases.title: Design Decision Documentation
use_cases.description: Teams documenting architectural decisions benefit from the structured approach that captures context, explores trade-offs, and validates clarity through reader testing.

use_cases.title: Product Requirements and Proposals
use_cases.description: Product teams can transform rough ideas into polished PRDs by following the context gathering, refinement, and testing stages to ensure stakeholder-ready output.

prompt_templates.title: Start Documentation Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to write a technical specification for a new feature. Can you guide me through the documentation co-authoring workflow?

prompt_templates.title: Edit Existing Document
prompt_templates.prompt: I have an existing design doc that needs improvement. Let's use the co-authoring workflow to refine it section by section.

prompt_templates.title: Reader Testing Session
prompt_templates.prompt: My document is mostly complete. Let's do the reader testing stage to find gaps before I share it with the team.

prompt_templates.title: Full Workflow from Scratch
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to create a proposal document from scratch. Guide me through all three stages: context gathering, refinement and structure, and reader testing.

output_examples.input: User wants to write a design doc for a new caching layer
output_examples.output: A structured workflow begins with context questions about the caching problem, audience, and constraints. After info-dumping and clarifying questions, the document is built section-by-section (problem statement, alternatives considered, proposed approach, implementation plan) with iterative refinement. Reader testing validates the doc catches key questions before team review.

output_examples.input: User has a draft RFC that feels incomplete
output_examples.output: The workflow identifies gaps through targeted questions, brainstorms missing considerations for each section, and produces a refined RFC. Reader testing with a fresh Claude instance surfaces ambiguities that are then addressed before final publication.

best_practices: Complete all three stages without skipping - each stage catches different types of issues
Provide thorough context during stage 1 to enable better guidance in later stages
Use reader testing feedback to identify genuine gaps rather than defending existing content
anti_patterns: Skipping context gathering and jumping straight to drafting results in incomplete documents
Making broad rewrites instead of surgical section-by-section refinements
Ignoring reader testing feedback because the content seems obvious to the author

faq.question: How long does the full workflow take?
faq.answer: The complete three-stage workflow typically takes 30-60 minutes for a substantial document. You can pause between stages and resume later. For shorter documents, the process may take 15-20 minutes.

faq.question: Do I need to answer every clarifying question?
faq.answer: No, you can use shorthand responses, skip questions that dont apply, or indicate when context was already provided. The questions are guides, not requirements. Answer what helps move the document forward.

faq.question: Can I use this workflow without Claude Code file editing capabilities?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow adapts to environments without file editing by working with shared documents or having you copy output. Reader testing can be done manually by opening a fresh Claude conversation.

faq.question: What types of documents work best with this approach?
faq.answer: This workflow excels for substantial documents like technical specs, design docs, decision records, RFCs, and proposals. For quick notes or simple documentation, a freeform approach may be more efficient.

faq.question: How does reader testing work without sub-agent access?
faq.answer: Without sub-agents, you manually test by opening a fresh Claude conversation at claude.ai, pasting your document, and asking the predicted reader questions. Report back what the fresh Claude got right or wrong.

faq.question: Can I skip stages if Im in a hurry?
faq.answer: You can skip stages, but each stage serves a purpose: context gathering prevents rewrites, refinement ensures quality, and reader testing catches blind spots. If time-pressed, consider working freeform rather than partially using this workflow.
